schools.summary: Ellicott: the Hebrew is corrupt and the number lost; the LXX omit the verse, and 'Saul reigned one year' is only a conjectural paraphrase.
Gill surveys the readings—literal infant, an ellipsis of years, or the Targum's figure of a faultless one-year-old—without deciding.
Henry takes the plain sense (an uneventful first year) and warns the delay let the Philistines prepare, an instance of self-sufficiency leading to folly.
Clarke moves the clause to ch. 12 as a chronological note and begins the chapter 'when Saul had reigned two years.'

debates.question: What does 'Saul was the son of a year... he reigned two years' mean, given the defective Hebrew?

sides.position: the text is corrupt and the age simply lost
a plain chronological note—one quiet year, then the second year's events
figurative of innocence, following the Targum/Chaldee

schools.summary: Ellicott hails the first standing army in Israel and Saul's foresight; K&D note the verse follows the fixed regnal formula and introduces Jonathan.
Gill reads the 3000 as a personal guard plus a standing force to keep peace and watch the Philistines.
Clarke guesses Saul split the force to surprise three garrisons and capture the weapons his men lacked.
K&D locate Geba (modern Jeba) apart from Gibeah; Ellicott makes the word 'garrison' possibly a pillar or an officer and paints Jonathan as the ideal warrior, the 'gazelle.'
Gill distinguishes Geba from Gibeah and identifies the post with the Philistine garrison left after Samuel's victory.
Clarke reads a surprise attack by unarmed men and praises Jonathan; Wesley makes the trumpet a call by messengers to gather for defence.

debates.question: Was Saul's trumpet a war-summons or a spreading of the news?

sides.position: a summons to prepare at once for war
messengers reporting Jonathan's deed and the Philistines' rage

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'Saul' stands for Jonathan the general-in-chief; the ancient enmity from Joshua's conquest is roused, and the people gather slowly and disperse quickly.
Gill sees a concerted plan to clear the garrisons, but faults Saul for having disbanded his large army beforehand.
Wesley suspects a broken treaty behind the 'abomination'; Clarke calls the blow the start of war and the shaking off of the Philistine yoke.
Ellicott marshals army-rolls (Jabin, Pharaoh, Solomon, Zerah) to show 30,000 chariots is impossible; the true reading is likely 300, the figures untrustworthy.
Clarke emends 'thirty thousand' to 'three thousand' on the Syriac and Arabic; Wesley takes the chariots as mostly baggage carriages, the allies all joined.
Gill reads the muster as Philistia's preemptive strike against a people bent on casting off their yoke.

debates.question: How is the impossible figure of 30,000 chariots to be handled?

sides.position: mostly baggage carriages, not war chariots, swelled by allies

schools.summary: K&D detail the hiding-places and locate the scene at the Jordan-valley Gilgal; Ellicott stresses the allied host and reads 'high places' as towers/holds, not shrines.
Gill sets the distress against an overwhelming host that expected nothing but destruction, noting Abarbanel's alternative reading.
Wesley: the strait exposed the vanity of trusting a king; Clarke: unarmed and few, they fled to the caves in terror.
K&D read verse and context together: Saul lingers while his trembling army melts, and argue the Gilgal here is the one down in the Jordan valley.
Gill traces the flight beyond Jordan, with Kimchi, to the farthest and (as it once proved) safest ground, where Ammon had been beaten.
Henry: Saul sinned by usurping sacrifice and spurning direction, then boasted instead of repenting—'not sinning but not repenting' ruins men.
Clarke: Saul fused prophet, priest and king and so attacked the constitution; Wesley notes the seventh day was not complete.
Ellicott: the charge of 1 Sam. 10:8 had been repeated for this occasion, and Saul's faith failed him under the pressure of his melting army.

debates.question: Wherein lay the gravity of Saul's sin?

sides.position: unlawful assumption of priestly office, confounding the three sacred offices
unbelief and impatience—a failure of faith under pressure, disobeying a positive command

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott deny Saul offered with his own hand—a priest (Ahiah) officiated, as with David and Solomon; the sin lies in the timing, not usurped priesthood.
Clarke calls the offering 'most perfectly unconstitutional'—Saul had no authority to offer or cause to be offered any sacrifice.
Gill reads Saul's motive: impatient, watching desertion, unwilling to fight before sacrificing and imploring God's help.

debates.question: Did Saul's sin consist in usurping the priest's office?

sides.position: no—a priest officiated; the sin was the premature, disobedient timing

schools.summary: K&D give Saul's self-justifying reply and note 'entreated the face of Jehovah' echoes Exod. 32:11; Ellicott reads Saul's public greeting as genuine, lifelong reverence for Samuel.
Clarke laments the fatal precipitation: one more hour would have prevented every evil and lost no good.
Gill notes Samuel came before the peace offerings could be made—Saul's haste outran even its own ritual.
Clarke itemizes Saul's three excuses and answers that Samuel came within the time; Wesley grants the day was far spent yet leaves the disobedience standing.
Ellicott reads the excuses as a preference to act without divine blessing—forgetting the Judges' deliverances—an overt rebellion; K&D show the doom's suppressed conditionals.
Gill: Samuel's question aims to draw out confession, though the prophet already knew the deed by the Spirit.
Clarke: Saul was sincere but rash, overriding the precept that the Lord should tell him what to do; Wesley reads 'I forced myself' as acting against his own conscience, sacrifice implying prayer.
Gill notes Saul 'said… within himself'—this is the inward calculation by which he reasoned past the command.
Gill: Saul is charged not with usurping the priesthood but with not waiting—impatience, disregard of Samuel, and distrust of God—rebuked boldly though he was king.
Wesley: 'for ever' means a long, generational stability the dynasty might have had, not the displacing of Judah's destined throne.
Clarke: 'after God's own heart' describes David's official, not private, conduct—a defence against infidels; Wesley stresses the sentence was conditional, the final rejection coming only at ch. 15.
Ellicott: David was still a child; the threat was conditional, no fatalism, yet foreknown; K&D note Saul mustered only 600, his sacrifice having gained nothing.
Gill: the kingdom is to pass from Saul's family and tribe altogether.

debates.question: Was the sentence on Saul final and just for so small a fault?

sides.position: conditional and not yet final—repentance was still open until ch. 15
just, because disobedience to an express command is no little sin—'no little god to sin against'

themes: a man after God's own heart

schools.summary: Henry: Saul is 'left to himself,' yet Samuel's going toward Gibeah shows he had not wholly abandoned him—still praying for the king he would not pray with.
K&D: the unseasonable sacrifice failed even to hold the army; Ellicott sees divine punishment already begun as only 600 answer the roll-call.
Clarke: Samuel accompanies Saul to give needful help, for Saul is not to be removed during his life—and not a sword or spear among the six hundred.
Ellicott corrects 'Gibeah' to 'Geba of Benjamin,' a strong pass-position for watching the enemy; K&D mark v.16 as the start of a new section on the raids and disarming before ch. 14's victory.
Gill: Saul and Jonathan are now joined to their men after the diminished muster.

debates.question: Is the place Gibeah or Geba of Benjamin?

sides.position: Geba—the translators erred, misled by v.15
Gibeah, as the received text reads

schools.summary: K&D map the three raiding routes (Ophrah/land of Shual, etc.); Ellicott calls this Saul's lowest ebb, borrowing Stanley's image of the king weeping over his ruined land.
Gill: the unchecked raids proved the vanity of trusting a king and were suffered to turn Israel's trust back to the Lord.
Clarke: the Philistines split into three bands to pillage, a dispersal Jonathan would soon exploit.
K&D read the three raiding routes as a deliberate fanning-out and join the verse to the note that no smith remained—the raids and the disarming are one calamity.
Gill fixes Beth-horon, upper and nether, in Ephraim, northwest of the Philistine camp at Michmash.
Clarke: the Philistines carried off the smiths as Porsenna did to the Romans and Nebuchadnezzar to the Jews—a familiar instrument of subjugation.
Ellicott: allow a year or more of unchecked raids; the ruined smithies were chiefly in the south, Benjamin, the king's recruiting-ground.
Gill: the Philistines removed the smiths into their own country and forbade the trade in Israel.
Wesley: 'down to the Philistines' means their garrisons within Israel, where the smiths were kept; Clarke: they were denied even the making of farm tools that Porsenna allowed Rome.
Ellicott parses the disputed tool-names (coulter/ploughshare, mattock/hoe); K&D read on to the swordless day of battle and resolve the tension with the Ammon victory.
Gill: Israel was obliged to go to the Philistine garrisons where the smith's trade was exercised.

themes: sharpening tools at the enemy's forge

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'file' (Targum, Rashi) has yielded to Gesenius's 'bluntness'—the tools grew dull for want of a smith; the LXX read it as the price of sharpening.
Clarke traces petsirah to 'rub hard,' guessing a grindstone to restore blunted edges, though the versions differ widely.
Gill: some kept files at home to sharpen their tools, others went to the Philistines when the edges were blunt.

debates.question: Does the obscure word mean 'a file' or 'bluntness'?

schools.summary: Clarke and Wesley qualify the 'no sword nor spear': bows and slings needed no smith and swords were hidden; the want was of new-forged close-combat arms among the six hundred.
Ellicott: the words are not to be pressed literally—the lack was in the southern valleys, the king's recruiting-ground, while Philistia armed its men to the teeth.
Gill sets the scene of the day of reckoning, when Israel must fight, flee, or surrender.

debates.question: Was Israel literally without any arms but Saul's and Jonathan's?

sides.position: not literally—swords were hidden, and bows and slings abounded
true especially of the southern recruiting-districts, not the whole nation

themes: the frame of a coming miracle

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott read the verse as an introduction to Jonathan's exploit—a Philistine outpost pushed to the pass to guard against surprise; K&D detail the precipitous Wady es Suweinit.
Gill weighs the readings (Jarchi, Targum's 'governor,' Kimchi's 'generals'), preferring a garrison guarding a strong rocky pass near Michmash.
